content: just wondering, how can I tell the target rustc version for a given tree ?

Since v6.11, the kernel supports a minimum, rather than a fixed version.

To find the minimum please see the table at https://docs.kernel.org/process/changes.html, or you can run scripts/min-tool-version.sh rustc.

I hope that helps!
Thanks! Kind of weirded out because I am trying to build the 6.11 kernel with 1.85 2025-11-27 rustc
And it fails due to unknown feature receiver_trait

Yeah, since we still use unstable features, it is possible to hit an issue in older kernels with newer Rust compilers.

Note that v6.11 will not be supported for much longer (that particular issue is fixed in v6.13-rc1, and we are going to send it to v6.12 LTS).
Thanks a lot, I was attempting to switch to 13 right now so that's good to hear

To be more precise, I edited above: 6.11 is about to stop being supported (there will be one more release, but that is probably the last one).

For development, you should use Linus' latest tags or rust-next etc. Users should move to v6.12 LTS.

Alexandros Liarokapis said:

Thanks a lot, I was attempting to switch to 13 right now so that's good to hear

You're welcome! Thanks for mentioning it.

Hello there. So I just ended up in the situation that a newer rustc version is not able to compile the kernel at an a older commit (6.12-rc8 works 1.83 but not with 1.84, also due to receiver_trait). I thought I'd share here that 1.83 works.

In general, old commits and tags will not work with compilers that were in the future. Also, please note that old tags are not supported (i.e. by the kernel in general, not just us).

Instead, what is supported are stable kernel branches, and for those we try our best to keep them working with the newer Rust compilers that get released.
So, for instance, I recently tested v6.12.19 with Rust 1.78 and Rust 1.85.

Sorry for not being clear, I had cloned mainline around that time. So yes, old commit. Thanks that clarifies things for me. How is that support achieved, e.g. in the presence of unsafe features that get stabilized?

No worries, and you're welcome!

I am not sure exactly what you mean, but unstable features can be used even if they are stabilized. The major issue is really changes to those features that could break the kernel (or even removal of features).

We hedge against that by having the kernel in upstream Rust's CI -- so all PRs landing there are required to be pass a minimal kernel build of the Rust parts.

Moreover, we are collaborating with upstream Rust, so they know what we are doing; and anyway we don't allow random features to be enabled. In other words, it is a controlled set, and especially in terms of language features, it is getting very small now.

Even then, if something breaks, we can still workaround things etc. on our side, and we have some weeks until a nightly becomes beta and then a stable release. We test those on our side, and sometimes send preemptive cleanups even for things like Clippy.

I talk a bit about this in e.g. LPC 2024 and FOSDEM 2025.

For stable kernels, we backport whatever fixes or workarounds are needed from mainline, just like anything else. Of course, if one picks an old tag from those, then it may or may not work, just like if one picks an old mainline tag. But old tags are not supported anyway by the stable kernel team, i.e. one is not supposed to use them.

I hope that clarifies!
Thanks, it is helpful! Specifically, I did not know that it is possible to use unstable features after they are stabilized.

Even more specifically, I mean :sweat_smile: of course when it is stabilized, the functionality exists, but I was wondering about the feature gate. So my updated understanding:

  • first, rustc changes to that the functionality is usable without the feature
  • then, the feature string is removed.
    The second step would break code that mentions the feature string - but we can know about such a change happening and can remove the !#[feature(foobar)] lines from the source. When such a thing happens, and we keep on using the actual functionality, the minimum version necessary to build the kernel would need to be bumped.
The feature gate just emits a warning saying "this feature is stabilized so you don't need a feature gate anymore". We silence that warning.
